text: The surgeon carefully performs sharp dissection around the umbilical area where the incisional hernia is located, encountering scarred tissue. The dissection progresses slowly to avoid creating holes in the peritoneal sac, aiming to keep it intact to prevent tension during closure.

The surgeon carefully dissects above and below the hernia across the midline, aiming to reduce the hernia sac while avoiding holes in the thin peritoneum. The dissection is performed on the posterior sheath and transversalis fascia, with gentle pressure applied to avoid grabbing the delicate tissue.

The surgeon continues the dissection on the opposite side of the hernia, attempting to recruit additional peritoneum despite the presence of holes, to ensure a more redundant tissue closure. The procedure involves using a fifteen by fifteen mesh.

The surgeon continues the dissection on the contralateral side, progressing through the tissue plane with blunt dissection. The peritoneal flap is gently pushed down without grabbing, aiming to create a larger pocket than the standard fifteen by fifteen mesh size. This approach ensures the flap can be brought across the ipsilateral side without tension, effectively covering the mesh outside the peritoneal defect.

The surgeon continues to expand the dissection area before proceeding to sew the posterior peritoneal hole closed, ensuring the closure is perpendicular to avoid shortening the flap. The hernia defect is then closed with an imbrication technique applied to the dermis.

The surgeon decreases insufflation pressure to eight for closure of smaller defects, using a fifteen by fifteen mesh with trio silk tied in the middle. Sutures are placed in all four corners of the mesh to prevent clamshelling or folding, and the flap is sewn closed.

The surgeon adjusts the port placement, sewing close to the midline while pulling the peritoneal flap to the ipsilateral side to ensure the 15x15 mesh remains covered by peritoneum. Sub-centimeter holes are closed with 3-0 Vicryl sutures to prevent mesh exposure to the abdominal cavity.
